title: OOF horizon distillation plus sigmoid-calibrated blending
summary: Lap-level pit-stop papers and RUL literature both report gains from jointly predicting the event and its remaining horizon.
rationale: Lap-level pit-stop papers and RUL literature both report gains from jointly predicting the event and its remaining horizon. Even if you do not train a full multitask net, an auxiliary out-of-fold horizon model can provide a strong distilled feature; calibrated OOF scores then make cross-family blending less brittle.
implementation_hint: Train an auxiliary OOF model for `laps_to_next_pit` or `P(stop within k laps)` for small k. Feed those OOF predictions into the main binary model, and when ensembling tree/survival/ranking branches, apply fold-safe sigmoid calibration to each branch before weighted blending so their scales are more comparable while preserving rank order.
expected_effect: Usually improves the final blend more than any single branch alone, especially if the new models capture different failure modes around late-stint decisions.
risk: Strict OOF generation is mandatory; otherwise the distilled horizon features leak heavily. Calibration itself should not raise single-model AUC, so gains depend on ensemble complementarity.
